questions.question:
The probability that it will rain today is 0.5. The probability that it will rain tomorrow is 0.6. The probability that it will rain either today or tomorrow is 0.7. What is the probability that it will rain today and tomorrow?

A. 0.3
B. 0.25
C. 0.35
D. 0.4

understand.plain: We know P(today), P(tomorrow), and P(today OR tomorrow). The inclusion-exclusion formula connects these four values: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B). Rearranging gives P(A and B) directly. The answer is 0.5 + 0.6 - 0.7 = 0.4.

keywords.explain: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B) avoids double-counting the overlap.
keywords.example: P(rain today or tomorrow) = 0.5 + 0.6 - P(both) = 0.7, so P(both) = 0.4.

keywords.explain: P(A and B) is the probability that both events happen simultaneously.
keywords.example: P(rain today AND tomorrow) = 0.4 means 40% chance both days are rainy.

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping circles labelled Today (0.5) and Tomorrow (0.6) with union 0.7 and intersection 0.4.

given.aim: Find P(rain today AND rain tomorrow).

terms.meaning: Probability it rains today.
terms.example: Half the time today is rainy.
terms.connects: One part of the inclusion-exclusion sum.

terms.meaning: Probability it rains tomorrow.
terms.example: 60% chance tomorrow is rainy.
terms.connects: Second part of the inclusion-exclusion sum.

terms.meaning: Probability that at least one of the two days is rainy.
terms.example: 70% chance there is rain on today or tomorrow or both.
terms.connects: Known union used to isolate the intersection.

given.plan: Apply inclusion-exclusion: P(A and B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A or B) = 0.5 + 0.6 - 0.7 = 0.4.

steps.title: Write inclusion-exclusion
steps.formula_raw: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)
steps.note: Rearranging isolates the unknown joint probability.

steps.title: Solve for joint probability
steps.formula_raw: P(A and B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A or B)
steps.note: The answer is 0.4, matching option D.

questions.formula_note: Inclusion-exclusion for two events directly yields the joint probability when the union and individual probabilities are known.

questions.question:
A die is rolled three times. The probability that exactly one odd number turns up among the three outcomes is

A. 1/8
B. 3/8
C. 5/8
D. 3/4

understand.plain: A standard die has three odd faces (1, 3, 5) and three even faces (2, 4, 6), so each roll independently gives odd or even with probability 1/2 each. We need exactly one odd in three rolls, which is a binomial probability with n=3, k=1, p=1/2. The result is C(3,1)*(1/2)^3 = 3/8.

keywords.explain: P(exactly k successes in n trials) = C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k) when each trial is independent.

keywords.term: Symmetry of odd/even on a die
keywords.explain: Exactly half the faces (1,3,5) are odd, so p(odd) = 1/2 per roll.
keywords.example: Rolling a 1, 3, or 5 counts as odd; rolling 2, 4, or 6 counts as even.

understand.visual_alt: Three dice rolls, exactly one shows an odd face while the other two show even faces.

given.aim: Find P(exactly one odd number in three rolls of a fair die).

terms.meaning: Number of independent die rolls.
terms.example: The die is thrown three times.
terms.connects: Number of trials in the binomial model.

terms.meaning: Probability of an odd outcome on each roll (3 odd faces out of 6).
terms.example: Rolling a 1, 3, or 5 is an odd result.
terms.connects: Success probability in the binomial formula.

terms.meaning: We want exactly one odd roll.
terms.example: Odd, Even, Even — one of three possible patterns.
terms.connects: The k in C(n,k).

steps.title: Identify p(odd) per roll
steps.apply: Three odd faces (1,3,5) out of 6, so p = 1/2.

steps.title: Apply binomial formula
steps.note: C(3,1) = 3 chooses which of the three rolls is the odd one.

questions.formula_note: Binomial counting C(3,1) picks which roll is odd, then (1/2)^3 gives the probability of that specific pattern.

questions.question:
Seven (distinct) car accidents occurred in a week. What is the probability that they all occurred on the same day?

A. 1/7
B. 1/7^6
C. 1/(2*7^6)
D. 7/2^6

understand.plain: Each accident independently falls on one of 7 days with equal probability 1/7. The total number of equally likely arrangements is 7^7. The favorable outcomes are those where all 7 accidents fall on the same day; there are exactly 7 such outcomes (all on Monday, all on Tuesday, etc.). So P = 7/7^7 = 1/7^6.

keywords.explain: Each accident is equally likely to happen on any of the 7 days, giving 7^7 total equally likely assignments.
keywords.example: Accident 1 on Monday, Accident 2 on Tuesday, etc. — each full assignment has probability (1/7)^7.

keywords.explain: All seven accidents on the same day: 7 ways (choose which day).
keywords.example: All on Monday, all on Tuesday, ..., all on Sunday — exactly 7 favorable assignments.

understand.visual_alt: Seven accident icons all pointing to a single day out of a seven-day week grid.

given.aim: Find P(all 7 accidents occur on the same day of the week).

terms.meaning: Seven distinct events, each independently assigned to a day.
terms.example: Accident A on day d_1, accident B on day d_2, etc.
terms.connects: Exponent in the sample space size 7^7.

terms.meaning: Each accident is equally likely on any of the 7 days.
terms.example: P(accident on Monday) = 1/7.
terms.connects: Base in 7^7 and in the 7 favorable outcomes.

terms.meaning: Total equally likely assignments of 7 accidents to 7 days.
terms.example: 7 choices for accident 1, 7 for accident 2, ..., 7 for accident 7.
terms.connects: Denominator of the probability fraction.

given.plan: Count favorable (all same day = 7) divided by total arrangements (7^7). Simplify to 1/7^6.

steps.title: Count total equally likely assignments
steps.apply: Each of the 7 accidents independently chooses 1 of 7 days: 7 * 7 * ... * 7 = 7^7.
steps.note: Assumes accidents are equally likely on each day and independent.

steps.title: Count favorable outcomes
steps.apply: All on Monday, all on Tuesday, ..., all on Sunday = 7 outcomes.
steps.note: There are exactly 7 ways for all accidents to share one day.

steps.title: Compute probability
steps.note: 7/7^7 simplifies by cancelling one factor of 7.

questions.formula_note: Product rule gives 7^7 total assignments; dividing 7 favorable by 7^7 cancels to 1/7^6.

questions.question:
If a fair coin is tossed four times. What is the probability that two heads and two tails will result?

A. 3/8
B. 1/2
C. 5/8
D. 3/4

understand.plain: Tossing a fair coin four times gives 2^4 = 16 equally likely outcomes. We need exactly 2 heads and 2 tails. The number of ways to choose which 2 of the 4 tosses are heads is C(4,2) = 6. So P = 6/16 = 3/8.

keywords.explain: The number of ways to pick 2 positions out of 4 for heads.
keywords.example: HHTT, HTHT, HTTH, THHT, THTH, TTHH — six arrangements.

keywords.explain: Four independent tosses each with 2 outcomes gives 2^4 = 16 equally likely sequences.
keywords.example: HHHH, HHHT, HHTH, ..., TTTT — 16 total sequences.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 340 120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='120' rx='8' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='170' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4 Coin Tosses: 2H 2T sequences</text><g transform='translate(20,38)'><rect width='280' height='28' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='140' y='19' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>HHTT  HTHT  HTTH  THHT  THTH  TTHH</text></g><text x='170' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>C(4,2) = 6 favorable out of 2^4 = 16 total</text><text x='170' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P = 6/16 = 3/8</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Six favorable sequences with exactly 2 heads and 2 tails out of 16 total, giving probability 3/8.

given.aim: Find P(exactly 2 heads and 2 tails in 4 fair coin tosses).

terms.meaning: Four independent coin flips, each with P(H) = P(T) = 1/2.
terms.example: One possible sequence: H, T, H, T.
terms.connects: Sample space size is 2^4 = 16.

terms.meaning: We want exactly 2 of the 4 tosses to be heads.
terms.example: The other 2 tosses must be tails.
terms.connects: k in the binomial count C(4,2).

terms.meaning: Number of ways to choose 2 positions out of 4 for heads.
terms.connects: Numerator of the probability.

steps.title: Count total outcomes
steps.note: All 16 sequences are equally likely for a fair coin.

steps.title: Count favorable outcomes
steps.apply: Choose which 2 of 4 positions are heads: C(4,2) = 6.
steps.note: The remaining 2 positions are automatically tails.

steps.title: Compute probability
steps.note: Simplify by dividing numerator and denominator by 2.

questions.formula_note: C(4,2) counts the favorable sequences and 2^4 is the equally likely sample space size.

questions.question:
Consider a company that assembles computers. The probability of a faulty assembly of any computer is p. The company therefore subjects each computer to a testing process. This testing process gives the correct result for any computer with a probability of q. What is the probability of a computer being declared faulty?

A. pq + (1-p)(1-q)
B. (1-q)p
C. (1-p)q
D. pq

understand.plain: A computer is declared faulty in two mutually exclusive scenarios: (1) it IS faulty and the test correctly identifies it as faulty, or (2) it is NOT faulty but the test incorrectly declares it faulty. The total probability is the sum of these two cases. Since the test is correct with probability q, it is incorrect with probability 1-q.

keywords.explain: P(declared faulty) = P(declared faulty | faulty)*P(faulty) + P(declared faulty | not faulty)*P(not faulty).
The test gives the correct answer with probability q; so P(says faulty | is faulty) = q and P(says not faulty | is not faulty) = q.
keywords.example: P(test error) = 1-q applies in both directions.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 360 150'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='150' rx='8' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='180' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Law of Total Probability</text><rect x='10' y='34' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Faulty (p)</text><text x='90' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Test correct (q) => declared faulty</text><rect x='190' y='34' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Not Faulty (1-p)</text><text x='270' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Test wrong (1-q) => declared faulty</text><text x='180' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P(declared faulty) = p·q + (1-p)·(1-q)</text><text x='180' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Answer: pq + (1-p)(1-q)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two branches: faulty computer tested correctly (p times q) and non-faulty computer tested incorrectly (1-p times 1-q), summing to the total probability of being declared faulty.

given.aim: Find P(computer is declared faulty by the test).

terms.meaning: Probability a randomly assembled computer is actually defective.
terms.example: If p=0.05, then 5% of computers have assembly faults.
terms.connects: Prior probability used in total probability calculation.

terms.meaning: Probability the test gives the right verdict for any computer.
terms.example: If q=0.9, the test is correct 90% of the time.
terms.connects: Determines both P(says faulty | is faulty) = q and P(says OK | is OK) = q.

terms.meaning: When the computer is truly faulty, the correct test result is 'faulty', occurring with probability q.
terms.example: Test correctly catches the defect with probability q.
terms.connects: First branch of total probability.

given.plan: Apply total probability: P(declared faulty) = P(faulty)*P(test says faulty | faulty) + P(not faulty)*P(test says faulty | not faulty) = p*q + (1-p)*(1-q).

steps.title: Identify the two disjoint paths to 'declared faulty'
steps.apply: Path 1: Computer IS faulty AND test says faulty. Path 2: Computer is NOT faulty AND test says faulty (test error).
steps.note: The two paths are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.

steps.title: Assign conditional probabilities
steps.formula_raw: P(declared faulty | faulty) = q;  P(declared faulty | not faulty) = 1 - q
steps.apply: Test is correct with prob q, so P(says faulty | is faulty) = q; test is wrong with prob 1-q, so P(says faulty | is not faulty) = 1-q.

questions.formula_note: The law of total probability partitions on whether the computer is actually faulty, summing both paths that lead to the test declaring it faulty.

questions.question:
What is the probability that divisor of 10^99 is a multiple of 10^96?

A. 1/625
B. 4/625
C. 12/625
D. 16/625

understand.plain: 10^99 = 2^99 * 5^99. Its divisors are all numbers of the form 2^a * 5^b where 0 <= a <= 99 and 0 <= b <= 99, giving 100*100 = 10000 divisors in total. A divisor 2^a * 5^b is a multiple of 10^96 = 2^96 * 5^96 exactly when a >= 96 and b >= 96. There are 4 choices for a (96,97,98,99) and 4 choices for b, giving 16 such divisors. P = 16/10000 = 1/625.

keywords.explain: 10^n = 2^n * 5^n; every divisor is 2^a * 5^b with 0 <= a,b <= n, giving (n+1)^2 total divisors.

keywords.term: Multiple condition in terms of exponents
keywords.explain: d = 2^a * 5^b is a multiple of 2^c * 5^e iff a >= c and b >= e.
keywords.example: 2^97 * 5^98 is a multiple of 2^96 * 5^96 because 97 >= 96 and 98 >= 96.

understand.visual_alt: A 100x100 grid of divisor pairs (a,b) with the 4x4 corner region a in {96..99}, b in {96..99} highlighted as the 16 favorable divisors.

given.aim: Find P(a uniformly random divisor of 10^99 is also a multiple of 10^96).

terms.meaning: Prime factorization of the number whose divisors we are choosing from.
terms.example: All divisors are 2^a * 5^b with 0 <= a,b <= 99.
terms.connects: Determines the sample space size.

terms.meaning: Number of equally likely divisors to pick from.
terms.example: 100 choices for the exponent of 2 times 100 choices for the exponent of 5.
terms.connects: Denominator of the probability.

terms.meaning: The divisibility threshold; a divisor must have at least this factor.
terms.example: We need a >= 96 and b >= 96.
terms.connects: Restricts favorable (a,b) pairs.

given.plan: Count favorable divisors (a in {96,97,98,99} and b in {96,97,98,99}) = 4*4 = 16. Divide by 10000. Simplify: 16/10000 = 1/625.

steps.title: Factor 10^99 and count all divisors
steps.note: Each divisor corresponds to a unique pair (a, b) with 0 <= a,b <= 99.

steps.title: Determine condition for multiple of 10^96
steps.formula_raw: 2^a * 5^b is divisible by 2^96 * 5^96  iff  a >= 96  and  b >= 96
steps.apply: a can be 96, 97, 98, or 99 (4 values); b can be 96, 97, 98, or 99 (4 values).

questions.formula_note: Prime factorization converts the divisor-counting problem into counting integer pairs (a,b) in a restricted range, then probability = favorable pairs / total pairs.

questions.question:
A deck of 52 cards (each carrying a distinct number from 1 to 52) is shuffled thoroughly. Two cards are then removed one at a time from the deck. What is the probability that the two cards are selected with the number on the first card being one higher than the number on the second card?

A. 1/52
B. 2/52
C. 1/4
D. 1/2

understand.plain: We draw two cards in order (first, then second) without replacement. The total number of ordered pairs is 52 * 51. For the first card to be exactly one higher than the second, the pair must be of the form (k+1, k) for some k from 1 to 51. There are exactly 51 such ordered pairs. So P = 51 / (52 * 51) = 1/52.

keywords.explain: Drawing two cards in sequence gives 52 choices for the first card and 51 remaining for the second, totaling 52*51 = 2652 equally likely ordered pairs.
keywords.example: (Drawing 5 then 3) and (drawing 3 then 5) are two different outcomes.

keywords.explain: First card = second card + 1 means pairs like (2,1), (3,2), ..., (52,51) — exactly 51 favorable ordered pairs.

understand.visual_alt: A number line from 1 to 52 where exactly 51 consecutive descending pairs (k+1, k) are the favorable outcomes out of 2652 ordered draws.

given.aim: Find P(first card drawn = second card drawn + 1) when two cards are drawn in order without replacement from a 52-card deck numbered 1-52.

terms.meaning: Each card has a unique integer label; order of drawing matters.
terms.example: Card 7 and card 7 cannot both be drawn.
terms.connects: Defines the sample space for ordered without-replacement draws.

terms.meaning: Number of equally likely ordered sequences of 2 distinct cards.
terms.example: (card 5 first, card 17 second) is one of the 2652 outcomes.
terms.connects: Denominator of the probability.

terms.meaning: Ordered pairs where first card = second card + 1; there are 51 such pairs.
terms.example: Pair (10, 9): first is 10, second is 9, and 10 = 9 + 1.
terms.connects: Numerator of the probability.

given.plan: Count favorable ordered pairs (51) and divide by total ordered pairs (2652 = 52*51). Simplify: 51/2652 = 1/52.

steps.title: Count total ordered draws
steps.apply: 52 choices for the first card, 51 remaining for the second: 52 * 51 = 2652.
steps.note: Drawing without replacement; order matters because the first card must be higher.

steps.title: Count favorable ordered pairs
steps.apply: k ranges from 1 to 51, giving 51 favorable pairs: (2,1),(3,2),...,(52,51).
steps.note: Exactly 51 consecutive descending pairs exist in a deck of 52.

questions.formula_note: Ordered sampling gives P(52,2)=2652 total outcomes; 51 consecutive-descending pairs are favorable, yielding 51/2652 = 1/52.
